title: Residential Panel Installation
description: A 200A residential electrical panel has been installed in the garage. Review the listed components and identify what's missing.
presentItems: 200A main breaker panel mounted on wall
Service entrance conductors terminated
Branch circuit breakers installed
Neutral bus bar with neutral conductors
Panel cover with directory card

missingItems.name: Panel circuit directory (completed and legible)
missingItems.explanation: Every circuit must be legibly identified as to its purpose. A blank or illegible directory card fails inspection and makes troubleshooting dangerous. Required by NEC 408.4.

missingItems.name: Working space clearance of 36 inches maintained
missingItems.explanation: A minimum of 36 inches of clear working space must be maintained in front of the panel, 30 inches wide and from floor to a height of 6.5 feet. This is a safety requirement for electricians. Required by NEC 110.26(A).

explanation: Residential panels require a completed circuit directory for safety and a clear working space for maintenance access. These are the two most commonly cited deficiencies during home inspections.

title: Receptacle Rough-In
description: A bedroom receptacle circuit has been roughed in during new construction. Identify what's missing from the installation.
presentItems: 14/2 NM-B cable run from panel
Single-gang plastic boxes nailed to studs
Cables stapled within 12 inches of each box
Cables supported every 4.5 feet along run
Ground wires connected in each box

missingItems.name: AFCI protection at the panel or first outlet
missingItems.explanation: All 120V, 15A and 20A branch circuits supplying outlets in dwelling unit bedrooms require arc-fault circuit interrupter protection. This can be a breaker-type or outlet-type AFCI. Required by NEC 210.12(A).

missingItems.name: Receptacle within 6 feet of each doorway
missingItems.explanation: Receptacles must be placed so that no point along the floor line of any wall space is more than 6 feet from a receptacle outlet. A doorway resets the measurement. Required by NEC 210.52(A).

explanation: Bedroom circuits require AFCI protection to detect dangerous arcing faults in wiring and cords. Proper receptacle spacing eliminates the need for extension cords, which are a leading cause of electrical fires.

title: Service Entrance Installation
description: A residential 200A overhead service entrance has been installed. Check for missing components.
presentItems: Weather head (service head) at top of mast
Service entrance cable to meter socket
Meter socket mounted on exterior wall
Service entrance conductors to main panel
200A main breaker panel inside

missingItems.name: Grounding electrode system (ground rods)
missingItems.explanation: The service entrance must be connected to a grounding electrode system. Typically two ground rods minimum 8 feet long, driven at least 6 feet apart, or a concrete-encased electrode (Ufer ground). Required by NEC 250.50 and 250.53.

missingItems.name: Grounding electrode conductor from panel to ground rods
missingItems.explanation: A properly sized grounding electrode conductor (GEC) must connect the neutral bus in the main panel to the grounding electrode system. For 200A service, a minimum #4 AWG copper GEC is required. Required by NEC 250.66, Table 250.66.

explanation: The grounding electrode system is the foundation of electrical safety. It provides a path for lightning and fault currents to dissipate into the earth. Without it, the entire electrical system is ungrounded and dangerous.

title: Commercial Lighting Installation
description: Fluorescent troffer lighting has been installed in a new commercial office space. Identify what's missing.
presentItems: 2x4 LED troffer fixtures in drop ceiling
277V branch circuits from lighting panel
Occupancy sensors in each room
Emergency battery packs in select fixtures
All fixtures properly supported from structure above

missingItems.name: Daylight harvesting controls near windows
missingItems.explanation: Energy codes (IECC/ASHRAE 90.1) require daylight-responsive controls for general lighting in primary sidelit zones (within 15 feet of windows). These controls dim artificial lights when natural daylight is sufficient. Required by ASHRAE 90.1-2019 Section 9.4.1.1.

missingItems.name: Exit signs at required egress points
missingItems.explanation: Illuminated exit signs are required at all exits and where the path of egress is not immediately apparent. They must be connected to the emergency circuit and have 90-minute battery backup. Required by NEC 700.16 and IBC 1013.

distractors: Dedicated neutral for each lighting circuit
explanation: Commercial lighting requires energy code compliance with daylight harvesting controls and life-safety exit illumination. Both are code requirements that are often overlooked during installation.

title: Motor Control Center
description: A motor control center (MCC) has been installed in an industrial facility mechanical room. Check for missing components.
presentItems: MCC lineup with vertical bus rated at 600A
Individual motor starter buckets with overloads
Disconnect switches in each bucket
Control power transformer for 120V control circuit
Phase and ground bus connections

missingItems.name: Arc-flash hazard labels on each section
missingItems.explanation: NFPA 70E and NEC 110.16 require arc-flash warning labels on equipment likely to require examination, adjustment, servicing, or maintenance while energized. Labels must include incident energy level or PPE category. Required by NEC 110.16 and NFPA 70E 130.5.

missingItems.name: Surge protective device (SPD) at MCC
missingItems.explanation: The NEC requires a surge protective device (Type 1 or Type 2 SPD) at the service equipment and has expanded this requirement. For motor control centers fed from the service, surge protection prevents damage to sensitive motor starters and VFDs. Required by NEC 242.24.

missingItems.name: Equipment grounding conductor termination bar
missingItems.explanation: Each motor circuit requires an equipment grounding conductor properly terminated at the MCC grounding bus. The MCC must have an adequately sized grounding bus bonded to the enclosure. Required by NEC 250.118 and 430.245.

explanation: Motor control centers are the hub of industrial electrical systems. Arc-flash labeling, surge protection, and proper grounding are all NEC requirements that protect both equipment and electricians working on the MCC.

title: Generator Transfer Switch Installation
description: A standby generator with automatic transfer switch has been installed at a commercial building. Identify missing components.
presentItems: 50kW natural gas standby generator on concrete pad
Automatic transfer switch (ATS) in main electrical room
Generator output conductors to ATS
ATS output to emergency panel
Weekly exercise timer programmed

missingItems.name: Remote annunciator panel at a constantly attended location
missingItems.explanation: For legally required standby systems, a remote annunciator panel must be installed at a location readily observed by personnel on duty. It must indicate generator status, fault conditions, and transfer switch position. Required by NEC 701.26.

missingItems.name: Battery charger and block heater for generator
missingItems.explanation: Standby generators must have a battery charger to maintain starting batteries and a block heater to keep the engine warm for reliable starting. Without these, the generator may fail to start when needed most. Required by NFPA 110 Section 5.6.

explanation: Emergency and standby generator systems require remote monitoring and engine maintenance accessories to ensure reliability. When the power goes out, the generator must start within 10 seconds every time.

title: EV Charger Installation
description: A Level 2 (240V, 50A) electric vehicle charger has been installed in a residential garage. Check for missing components.
presentItems: Level 2 EVSE unit mounted on wall
50A double-pole breaker in panel
6 AWG copper conductors in conduit to charger
NEMA 14-50 receptacle or hardwired connection

missingItems.name: GFCI protection for the EV charging circuit
missingItems.explanation: Receptacles installed in garages require GFCI protection. For EV charging equipment, the EVSE typically has built-in GFCI, but if using a receptacle, it must be GFCI protected. Required by NEC 210.8(A)(2) and 625.54.

missingItems.name: Ventilation consideration for battery charging
missingItems.explanation: While modern EVs have sealed battery systems, the NEC still requires that the electrical installation comply with manufacturer requirements. Adequate ventilation must be verified per the EVSE listing and installation instructions. Required by NEC 625.52.

explanation: EV charger installations combine standard electrical requirements (GFCI in garages) with specific NEC Article 625 requirements. As EV adoption grows, IBEW electricians must master these installations.

title: Fire Alarm System
description: A new fire alarm system has been installed in a commercial building. Review the installation for missing components.
presentItems: Fire alarm control panel (FACP) in lobby
Smoke detectors in corridors and rooms
Manual pull stations at exits
Horn/strobe notification appliances
Fire alarm wiring in red conduit

missingItems.name: Duct smoke detectors at air handling units over 2,000 CFM
missingItems.explanation: Duct smoke detectors are required in the supply duct of air handling systems over 2,000 CFM. They shut down the HVAC system upon detecting smoke to prevent smoke spread throughout the building. Required by NFPA 72 and IMC 606.4.

missingItems.name: Secondary power supply (batteries) at the FACP
missingItems.explanation: The fire alarm control panel must have a secondary power supply (standby batteries) capable of operating the system for 24 hours in standby mode plus 5 minutes of alarm. Required by NFPA 72 Section 10.6.7.

explanation: Fire alarm systems are life-safety installations that must function during power outages and HVAC emergencies. Duct smoke detectors and backup batteries are critical code requirements.

title: Emergency Lighting Installation
description: Emergency lighting has been installed in a commercial building corridor. Identify what's missing.
presentItems: Emergency light units with battery packs in corridors
Illuminated exit signs at exit doors
Emergency lighting on dedicated emergency circuit
Exit signs connected to generator backup

missingItems.name: 90-minute battery backup for emergency lights
missingItems.explanation: Emergency lighting must provide illumination for at least 90 minutes after a loss of normal power. Battery units must be tested monthly (30-second test) and annually (90-minute test). Required by NEC 700.12 and IBC 1008.3.

missingItems.name: Minimum 1 foot-candle illumination along egress path
missingItems.explanation: Emergency lighting must provide a minimum of 1 foot-candle of illumination at the floor level along the path of egress. Illumination must be measured at the darkest point along the route. Required by IBC 1008.3.4.

explanation: Emergency lighting is a life-safety system that must provide adequate illumination for safe building evacuation. Duration, illumination levels, and regular testing are all code requirements.

title: Transformer Installation
description: A 75 kVA dry-type transformer has been installed in a commercial electrical room to step down 480V to 208/120V. Check for missing components.
presentItems: 75 kVA dry-type transformer mounted on wall brackets
480V primary conductors in conduit
208/120V secondary conductors to panelboard
Primary overcurrent protection sized per NEC 450.3
Transformer grounded per NEC 250.30

missingItems.name: Secondary overcurrent protection
missingItems.explanation: When the primary overcurrent device is sized larger than 125% of the rated primary current (common with standard breaker sizes), secondary overcurrent protection is required at no more than 125% of the rated secondary current. Required by NEC 450.3(B).

missingItems.name: Separately derived system grounding electrode conductor
missingItems.explanation: A separately derived system (transformer secondary) requires its own grounding electrode conductor connected to the nearest grounding electrode. The neutral must be bonded to ground at the transformer or first disconnect only. Required by NEC 250.30(A).

explanation: Transformer installations create separately derived systems that require independent grounding and proper overcurrent protection on both primary and secondary sides per NEC 450 and 250.30.

title: Data/Communications Room
description: An electrical and data communications room has been built out in a new commercial building. Identify what's missing.
presentItems: Dedicated 20A circuits for equipment
Overhead cable tray for data cables
Equipment racks bolted to floor
HVAC cooling for equipment heat load
UPS system for critical equipment
Fire suppression system installed

missingItems.name: Dedicated panelboard with isolated ground bus
missingItems.explanation: Data rooms require clean power with isolated grounding to prevent electromagnetic interference. A dedicated panelboard with an insulated equipment grounding conductor bus reduces noise on sensitive electronic equipment. Required by NEC 250.146(D) and TIA-607.

missingItems.name: Telecommunications bonding backbone (TBB)
missingItems.explanation: A telecommunications bonding backbone must connect all telecommunications grounding busbars to the main grounding busbar. This equalizes potential across all data equipment and prevents ground loops. Required by TIA-607-C and NEC 250.94.

missingItems.name: Electrical protection for power over ethernet (PoE) circuits
missingItems.explanation: PoE circuits delivering power over data cables must comply with NEC Article 725 and 840 for proper conductor ampacity, bundle derating, and fire resistance of cables in plenums and risers. Required by NEC 725.144 and 840.160.

explanation: Data/communications rooms require specialized electrical installations including isolated grounding, telecommunications bonding, and proper PoE circuit considerations. These are increasingly critical as buildings become more connected.

title: Solar PV System Installation
description: A 10 kW rooftop solar photovoltaic system has been installed on a commercial building. Check for missing components.
presentItems: PV modules mounted on racking system
String inverter mounted near main panel
DC conductors in metallic conduit on roof
AC disconnect at inverter output
PV system connected to main panel with backfeed breaker

missingItems.name: Rapid shutdown system
missingItems.explanation: NEC 690.12 requires a rapid shutdown system that reduces PV system voltage to 80V or less within 30 seconds outside the array boundary when initiated. This protects firefighters working on the roof from DC shock hazards. Required by NEC 690.12.

missingItems.name: DC arc-fault circuit protection
missingItems.explanation: PV systems operating at 80V DC or greater must have DC arc-fault protection to detect and interrupt dangerous arcing in the DC wiring. DC arcs burn hotter and longer than AC arcs and are a significant fire risk on rooftops. Required by NEC 690.11.

missingItems.name: PV system warning labels and signage
missingItems.explanation: Multiple warning labels are required: at the main service disconnect (indicating PV power source), at all disconnects, at the inverter, and on the roof near the array. Labels must indicate maximum voltage and the presence of dual power sources. Required by NEC 690.56 and 705.10.

explanation: Solar PV installations have specific NEC Article 690 requirements for safety. Rapid shutdown protects firefighters, arc-fault protection prevents rooftop fires, and warning labels alert utility workers and first responders to the presence of dual power sources.

title: VFD Bypass Installation
description: A variable frequency drive has been installed on an HVAC supply fan motor. Review the installation for missing components per UCSB specifications.
presentItems: VFD properly sized for motor horsepower
Input line reactor for harmonic mitigation
Shielded VFD-rated cable to motor
VFD programmed with motor nameplate data
Control wiring from BAS to VFD

missingItems.name: VFD bypass disconnect switch
missingItems.explanation: UCSB specifications require a bypass disconnect that allows the motor to run directly across the line if the VFD fails. For critical HVAC equipment, the bypass ensures continued building ventilation during VFD maintenance or failure. The bypass must include a manual motor starter with overload protection.

missingItems.name: Earth fault protection in bypass circuit
missingItems.explanation: The bypass circuit must include ground fault (earth fault) protection to detect insulation failure in the motor or cabling when operating in bypass mode. Without VFD monitoring, the bypass circuit relies on this dedicated protection to prevent equipment damage and electrical hazards.

explanation: VFD bypass arrangements are required by UCSB specs for critical HVAC equipment to maintain ventilation during drive failures. The bypass must include its own protective devices since the VFD's built-in protections are not active in bypass mode.

title: VFD Power Quality Compliance
description: A large VFD installation on a chilled water pump has been commissioned. Check for missing power quality components per UCSB and IEEE 519 requirements.
presentItems: 18-pulse VFD with phase-shifting transformer
Output dV/dt filter for motor protection
Shielded cable with grounding at both ends
VFD configured for sensorless vector control
BACnet communication module installed

missingItems.name: Power quality meter for THD monitoring at point of common coupling
missingItems.explanation: IEEE 519-2022 and UCSB specifications require total harmonic distortion to remain below 3% at the point of common coupling. A permanently installed power quality meter is needed to verify compliance and monitor ongoing harmonic levels. Without it, THD violations go undetected and can damage other equipment on the electrical bus.

missingItems.name: Active harmonic filter or additional harmonic mitigation
missingItems.explanation: UCSB specifications mandate IEEE 519 compliance at 3% THD maximum. Even with an 18-pulse drive, system conditions may push THD above the limit. An active harmonic filter provides real-time harmonic cancellation to ensure continuous compliance under all operating conditions.

explanation: UCSB specifications enforce strict IEEE 519 harmonic compliance at 3% THD. Large VFD installations require ongoing power quality monitoring and may need active harmonic filtering beyond the base drive configuration to maintain compliance under varying load conditions.
